author: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy
book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

lineage: Writings in Connection with the Manichæan Controversy.
Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.

The Beauty of the Straight Line Might Be Taken from the Region of Darkness Without Taking Anything from Its Substance. So Evil Neither Takes from Nor Adds to the Substance of the Soul. The Straightness of Its Side Would Be So Far a Good Bestowed on the Region of Darkness by God the Creator.

title: The Beauty of the Straight Line Might Be Taken from the Region of Darkness Without Taking Anything from Its Substance. So Evil Neither Takes from Nor Adds to the Substance of the Soul. The Straightness of Its Side Would Be So Far a Good Bestowed on the Region of Darkness by God the Creator.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: What is to be done with unhappy minds, perverse in error, and held fast by custom?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: These men do not know what they say when they say those things; for they do not consider.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Listen to me; no one forces you, no one quarrels with you, no one taunts you with past errors, unless some one who has not experienced the divine mercy in deliverance from error: all we desire is that the errors should some time or other be abandoned.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Think a little without animosity or bitterness.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: We are all human beings: let us hate, not one another, but errors and lies.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Think a little, I pray you.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: God of mercy, help them to think, and kindle in the minds of inquirers the true light.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If anything is plain, is not this, that right is better than wrong?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Give me, then, a calm and quiet answer to this, whether making crooked the right line of the region of darkness which joins on to the right line of the region of light, would not detract from its beauty.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: If you will not be dogged, you must confess that not only is beauty taken from it by its being made crooked, but also the beauty which it might have had from connection with the right line of the region of light.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Is it the case, then, that in this loss of beauty, in which right is made crooked, and harmony becomes discord, and agreement disagreement, there is any loss of substance?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Learn, then, from this that substance is not evil; but as in the body, by change of form for the worse, beauty is lost, or rather lessened, and what was called fair before is said to be ugly, and what was pleasing becomes displeasing, so in the mind the seemliness of a right will, which makes a just and pious life, is injured when the will changes for the worse; and by this sin the mind becomes miserable, instead of enjoying as before the happiness which comes from the ornament of a right will, without any gain or loss of substance.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Consider, again, that though we admit that the border of the region of darkness was evil for other reasons, such as that it was dim and dark, or any other reason, still it was not evil in being straight.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: So, if I admit that there was some evil in its color, you must admit that there was some good in its straightness.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Whatever the amount of this good, it is not allowable to attribute it to any other than God the Maker, from whom we must believe that all good in whatsoever nature comes, if we are to escape deadly error.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: It is absurd, then, to say that this region is perfect evil, when in its straightness of border is found the good of not a little beauty of a material kind; and also to make this region to be altogether estranged, from the almighty and good God, when this good which we find in it can be attributed to no other but the author of all good things.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But this border, too, we are told, was evil.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Well, suppose it evil: it would surely have been worse had it been crooked instead of straight.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And how can that be the perfection of evil than which something worse than itself can be thought of?

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And to be worse implies that there is some good, the want of which makes the thing worse.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Here the want of straightness would make the line worse.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: Therefore its straightness is something good.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: And you will never answer the question whence this goodness comes, without reference to Him from whom we must acknowledge that all good things come, whether small or great.

verses.book: Against the Epistle of Manichæus, Called Fundamental.
verses.text: But now we shall pass on from considering this border to something else.
